content.text: Before text can be embedded and stored in a vector store, it must be extracted from its source format. Parsing is the first—and often most error-prone—step in the RAG indexing pipeline. A bad parse propagates garbage text into every downstream step: chunking, embedding, and retrieval all silently degrade.

attrs.alt: Document parsing pipeline: source formats (PDF, HTML, DOCX, Markdown, OCR, Unstructured) → text extraction layer → cleaning and normalisation → metadata attachment → Document(page_content, metadata) ready for chunking

content.text: The pipeline has three stages for every format:
so the chunk can be traced back to its source.

PDFs are the most common enterprise document format and the most difficult to parse reliably. A PDF is a layout-first format: it encodes the position of every character on the page, not a logical text stream. Paragraph order, column boundaries, and table cell membership must all be inferred.
Best for tables and structured layouts
Verbose API; slowest of the four
When a PDF contains structured tables, pdfplumber's table detection is more reliable than plain text extraction. It returns rows as lists of strings:

# pip install pdfplumber
import pdfplumber, json

with pdfplumber.open("financial_tables.pdf") as pdf:
    for page_num, page in enumerate(pdf.pages):
        # Extract plain text for paragraphs
        text = page.extract_text()

        # Extract any tables on this page
        for table_idx, table in enumerate(page.extract_tables()):
            # table is list[list[str | None]]
            header = table[0]
            rows = table[1:]
            print(f"Page {page_num+1}, Table {table_idx}: {header}")
            for row in rows[:3]:
                print(" ", row)

# pip install pymupdf
import fitz   # pymupdf

doc = fitz.open("complex_layout.pdf")
pages_text = []
for page in doc:
    # get_text("blocks") returns (x0, y0, x1, y1, text, block_no, block_type)
    blocks = page.get_text("blocks")
    # sort top-to-bottom, left-to-right for reading order
    blocks.sort(key=lambda b: (round(b[1] / 10), b[0]))
    text = "
".join(b[4].strip() for b in blocks if b[4].strip())
    pages_text.append({"text": text, "page": page.number + 1})

doc.close()
A scanned PDF contains images of pages with no embedded text. pypdf returns an empty string. You need OCR (optical character recognition) to convert the image pixels to text:

# pip install pytesseract pillow pdf2image
# brew install tesseract poppler   (macOS) / apt-get install tesseract-ocr poppler-utils
import pytesseract
from pdf2image import convert_from_path

pages = convert_from_path("scanned_contract.pdf", dpi=300)
texts = []
for page_num, img in enumerate(pages):
    text = pytesseract.image_to_string(img, lang="eng")
    texts.append({"text": text, "page": page_num + 1, "source": "scanned_contract.pdf"})

# For production, prefer cloud OCR for higher accuracy:
# AWS Textract, Google Document AI, or Azure Document Intelligence

Tesseract accuracy drops on low-resolution scans, handwriting, or non-Latin scripts. AWS Textract and Azure Document Intelligence provide structured table and form extraction in addition to text, and handle these cases far better.
HTML documents contain the target content mixed with navigation, ads, footers, cookie banners, and JavaScript. The parsing job is to extract the signal and discard the noise.

# pip install beautifulsoup4 lxml requests
import requests
from bs4 import BeautifulSoup

resp = requests.get("https://example.com/article", timeout=10)
resp.raise_for_status()

soup = BeautifulSoup(resp.text, "lxml")

# Target the main article content — inspect the page to find the right selector
article = soup.select_one("article") or soup.select_one("main") or soup.body

# Remove boilerplate elements in-place
for tag in article.select("nav, header, footer, script, style, .cookie-banner, aside"):
    tag.decompose()

# Extract clean text — get_text with separator preserves paragraph breaks
text = article.get_text(separator="
", strip=True)

metadata = {
    "source": resp.url,
    "title": soup.title.string if soup.title else "",
    "content_type": "html",
}
print(text[:500])
trafilatura specialises in extracting the main readable content from any web page with no CSS selector configuration needed:

# pip install trafilatura
import trafilatura

html = trafilatura.fetch_url("https://example.com/article")
text = trafilatura.extract(
    html,
    include_comments=False,
    include_tables=True,
    no_fallback=False,           # use fallback extraction if main fails
    output_format="txt",         # or "xml" for structured output
)
# text is clean prose with boilerplate removed
If you want to chunk on HTML heading boundaries (h1/h2/h3) rather than character count, keep the heading structure during extraction:
DOCX files (Microsoft Word's XML format) have a well-structured XML schema. python-docx exposes paragraphs, headings, and tables through a clean API — no layout inference needed.

from docx import Document

doc = Document("financial_summary.docx")
for table_idx, table in enumerate(doc.tables):
    rows = []
    for row in table.rows:
        cells = [cell.text.strip() for cell in row.cells]
        rows.append(cells)

    # rows[0] is often the header
    print(f"Table {table_idx}: {rows[0]}")
    for row in rows[1:4]:     # first 3 data rows
        print(" ", row)
Unstructured: One Library for All Formats
library auto-detects the file format and delegates to the best parser for each type. It returns a list of typed
(Title, NarrativeText, Table, ListItem, Header, Footer) rather than a raw string, making downstream filtering trivial:

# pip install "unstructured[pdf,docx,html]"
from unstructured.partition.auto import partition
from unstructured.staging.base import elements_to_dicts

# Works for PDF, DOCX, HTML, PPTX, EML, EPUB, CSV, JSON, XML, ...
elements = partition(filename="company_overview.pdf")

# Filter to narrative content only (drops headers, footers, page numbers)
from unstructured.documents.elements import NarrativeText, Title, Table

text_elements = [e for e in elements if isinstance(e, (NarrativeText, Title))]
table_elements = [e for e in elements if isinstance(e, Table)]

# Convert to LangChain Documents
from langchain_core.documents import Document

docs = [
    Document(
        page_content=str(e),
        metadata={
            "source": "company_overview.pdf",
            "element_type": type(e).__name__,
            "page_number": e.metadata.page_number,
        },
    )
    for e in text_elements
]
print(f"{len(docs)} narrative elements extracted")

Unstructured also provides a hosted API with enhanced accuracy, especially for PDFs with complex tables and scanned content. The API partitions and cleans the document server-side; you receive the typed element list without any local dependency management.
Raw extracted text consistently has the same problems regardless of source format. A cleaning pass before chunking prevents these artifacts from corrupting embeddings:

import re
import unicodedata

def clean_text(text: str) -> str:
    """Normalise extracted text for embedding."""
    # 1. Normalise Unicode (NFC: compose combining characters)
    text = unicodedata.normalize("NFC", text)

    # 2. Fix common PDF ligature artifacts (ﬁ→fi, ﬂ→fl, etc.)
    ligatures = {"ﬁ": "fi", "ﬂ": "fl", "ﬀ": "ff", "ﬃ": "ffi", "ﬄ": "ffl",
                 "ﬅ": "st", "’": "'", "“": '"', "”": '"'}
    for bad, good in ligatures.items():
        text = text.replace(bad, good)

    # 3. Remove running headers/footers (repeated short lines at fixed positions)
    lines = text.splitlines()
    lines = [l for l in lines if len(l.strip()) > 4]   # drop stubs

    # 4. Collapse excessive whitespace
    text = "
".join(lines)
    text = re.sub(r"
{3,}", "

", text)     # max 2 consecutive newlines
    text = re.sub(r"[ 	]{2,}", " ", text)     # collapse spaces within lines

    # 5. Strip leading/trailing whitespace
    return text.strip()

# Apply to every extracted page
cleaned_docs = [
    {"text": clean_text(p["text"]), "metadata": {k: v for k, v in p.items() if k != "text"}}
    for p in pages_text
    if len(clean_text(p["text"])) > 50   # drop near-empty pages
]

Metadata attached at parse time travels with every chunk through splitting, embedding, and storage. It serves two purposes: source attribution in the final answer, and pre-filter conditions in vector store queries. Design it once at the loader stage.
PDF and DOCX; enables page-level citations
Heading the chunk falls under; aids context
earnings_release, policy, faq — enables metadata filtering
Route to language-specific retriever or index
paragraph, table, heading — filter by content type
Avoid adding metadata you cannot query on.
Every extra key adds noise to the metadata dict and marginally increases storage. Only add fields you will actually use in a filter or citation.
LangChain wraps all the parsers above behind a uniform

from langchain_community.document_loaders import (
    PyPDFLoader,             # pypdf under the hood, one Document per page
    UnstructuredHTMLLoader,  # unstructured for HTML
    Docx2txtLoader,          # docx2txt for DOCX (simple text extraction)
    UnstructuredFileLoader,  # auto-detect format
)

# PDF
pdf_docs = PyPDFLoader("report.pdf").load()
print(pdf_docs[0].metadata)
# {'source': 'report.pdf', 'page': 0}

# HTML (local file)
html_docs = UnstructuredHTMLLoader("page.html").load()

# DOCX
docx_docs = Docx2txtLoader("handbook.docx").load()

# Auto-detect (delegates to unstructured)
any_docs = UnstructuredFileLoader("mystery.pdf", mode="elements").load()
# mode="elements" returns one Document per structural element (Title, NarrativeText, …)
# mode="single"   returns the entire file as one Document
avoids loading all pages into memory for large PDFs. Call

# Process pages one at a time — useful for very large PDFs
loader = PyPDFLoader("large_corpus.pdf")
for doc in loader.lazy_load():
    # process doc.page_content, then discard
    process(doc)
Apply clean_text() before chunking; filter short lines
Check for empty text; fall back to pytesseract or cloud OCR
Ignoring column order in multi-column PDFs
Use pymupdf with block sorting or pdfplumber's layout detection
Use get_text(separator=' ') not strip(); preserve token boundaries
No source citations in final answers
Always set source, page_number at parse time
Nav links and cookie text dilute chunk quality
Use trafilatura or BeautifulSoup with targeted CSS selectors
Parsing is format-specific: PDF needs layout-aware extraction, HTML needs boilerplate removal, DOCX has a clean structural API.
pypdf is the easiest PDF parser; pdfplumber excels at tables; pymupdf is fastest and most accurate for complex layouts.
Scanned PDFs require OCR — check for empty page_content and fall back to pytesseract or cloud OCR (Textract, Document AI).
trafilatura removes web boilerplate automatically; BeautifulSoup with CSS selectors gives surgical control.
Clean text before chunking: fix ligatures, collapse whitespace, strip running headers/footers.
Metadata design matters: source, page_number, doc_type, and section_title unlock citations and vector store filtering downstream.
unstructured.io provides a single API for all formats and returns typed elements that make filtering straightforward.
